text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Staff Training and Shared Readiness lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Use these modules to build a common foundation before AI projects begin. The learning section can support staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, and cross-program discussion.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Use these modules to build a common foundation before AI projects begin. The learning section can support staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, and cross-program discussion.

text: Health departments can use the learning modules as practical training material rather than static background reading. The modules help staff, leaders, governance members, and program teams build a common foundation before AI projects begin.
textPreview: Health departments can use the learning modules as practical training material rather than static background reading. The modules help st...

text: Training should be connected to agency policy, approved tools, data safeguards, escalation expectations, and governance requirements.
textPreview: Training should be connected to agency policy, approved tools, data safeguards, escalation expectations, and governance requirements.

text:
Use this slide in the Staff Training and Shared Readiness training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and Tools 1, 2, 7, 23, 24, 28.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Training should be connected to agency policy, approved tools, data safeguards, escalation expectations, and governance requirements.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Staff Training and Shared Readiness in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Staff Training and Shared Readiness training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and Tools 1, 2, 7, 23, 24, 28.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

These learning modules are designed to help health departments create a shared baseline before AI projects begin. They can be used for staff onboarding, leadership orientation, governance preparation, cross-program training, or pre-work before readiness assessment and use case prioritization.

Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.
How Departments Can Use the Learning Modules
textPreview: How Departments Can Use the Learning Modules

text: Use the modules as self-paced reading before workshops or governance meetings. Use one module at a time as a 30- to 60-minute staff training session. Assign modules as pre-work before completing the readiness assessment or prioritizing AI use.
textPreview: Use the modules as self-paced reading before workshops or governance meetings. Use one module at a time as a 30- to 60-minute staff train...

text: Start with Understanding Artificial Intelligence, Generative AI, and Agentic AI so staff share basic vocabulary. Move into Risks, Human-Centered AI, Missing Voices, Public Transparency, and Tiered Data Use before use cases are selected. Use AI.
textPreview: Start with Understanding Artificial Intelligence, Generative AI, and Agentic AI so staff share basic vocabulary. Move into Risks, Human-C...

text: Start each session with a concrete public health workflow rather than a general technology discussion. Ask participants to identify what AI may support, what humans must decide, and what data safeguards are needed. End each session with a.

Use this slide in the Staff Training and Shared Readiness training module.
Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and Tools 1, 2, 7, 23, 24, 28.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with How Departments Can Use the Learning Modules, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.
textPreview: Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text:
Use this slide in the Staff Training and Shared Readiness training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and Tools 1, 2, 7, 23, 24, 28.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

Which staff groups need baseline AI training before tools are approved? Which modules should be required before governance participation, vendor review, or use case prioritization? How will the department document that staff understand data.

text: Create a short training plan that assigns modules to staff groups, identifies which exercises should be completed, and connects each module to a play or tool that the department will use next.

text:
Use this slide in the Staff Training and Shared Readiness training module.
Use this slide for a facilitated activity near the end of the lesson.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and Tools 1, 2, 7, 23, 24, 28.
Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Staff AI training plan, Module assignment matrix by role, Training completion expectations, List of governance or implementation artifacts produced during training.
Use this topic with Plays 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and Tools 1, 2, 7, 23, 24, 28. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.

Close by connecting the lesson to next actions and resources.

Point participants back to the playbook and toolkit resources page for additional references.
End by asking each participant or team to name one follow-up action they will take after the session.
